name: Scientology Fair Game
description: Scientology's "Fair Game" policy, originating in the 1960s and formally canceled in 1997, was a doctrine permitting and encouraging harassment of individuals and groups perceived as enemies of the Church of Scientology. According to former Scientologists and documents, the policy authorized actions such as lying, deceiving, suing, and even destroying such individuals. L. Ron Hubbard, the founder of Scientology, initially introduced the concept, defining "Suppressive Persons" (SPs) as individuals deemed to be a threat to Scientology. The policy allowed for actions against SPs that would otherwise be considered unethical or illegal. Proponents cite internal Scientology documents and testimonies from former members as evidence of the policy's existence and implementation. These sources detail specific instances of harassment, surveillance, and legal battles directed at critics and defectors. Mainstream scholarly perspectives largely condemn the Fair Game policy as a form of abuse and a violation of human rights. Scholars of religion and sociology have analyzed the policy within the context of Scientology's organizational structure and its perceived need to defend itself against external threats. Legal battles and journalistic investigations have further exposed the alleged tactics employed under the Fair Game doctrine, contributing to public criticism of Scientology. While the official policy was reportedly canceled, critics argue that similar tactics continue to be used against those who oppose Scientology.
subtitle: Harassment policy against Scientology's 'enemies'

claims.claim: Fair Game authorized unethical actions against perceived enemies.
claims.evidence: Internal Scientology directives and testimonies of former members detail sanctioned harassment, surveillance, and legal attacks.
claims.criticism: Scientology denies the widespread application of Fair Game and claims that any actions taken were justified self-defense against malicious attacks.

claims.claim: The policy targeted individuals who criticized or left Scientology.
claims.evidence: Numerous accounts from former Scientologists and journalists document instances of harassment and intimidation directed at critics and defectors.
claims.criticism: Scientology asserts that these individuals were actively attempting to harm the organization and that its actions were necessary to protect its members and beliefs.

claims.claim: Fair Game constituted a violation of human rights.
claims.evidence: Legal scholars and human rights organizations have condemned the policy as a form of abuse and a suppression of freedom of speech.
claims.criticism: Scientology argues that its actions were protected under religious freedom and that criticisms are often based on misinformation and bias.

keyFigures.name: L. Ron Hubbard
keyFigures.role: Founder of Scientology and originator of Fair Game.

keyFigures.name: Lawrence Wright
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Going Clear,' a critical examination of Scientology that details Fair Game.

keyFigures.name: Mike Rinder
keyFigures.role: Former senior executive in Scientology who has publicly criticized the organization and its practices, including Fair Game.

mythologyConnections: Demonization of dissenters (parallels to religious heresy narratives)
Us vs. Them mentality (common in many mythologies and belief systems)

timeline.event: L. Ron Hubbard introduces the concept of 'Suppressive Person' and the Fair Game policy.
Fair Game policy is formally codified within Scientology's internal directives.
Scientology officially cancels the Fair Game policy, though critics dispute its actual cessation.

sources.author: Lawrence Wright
